_provenance: Claude-authored original CET-6 careful reading, batch E5 (completes package E to 50). ~280-340 word academic/social passages, 4 MCQs each. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: Can reading fiction make us better people? It is an old and appealing idea — that literature, by carrying us inside the minds of others, cultivates our capacity for empathy. In recent years, this once-romantic notion has begun to attract scientific attention, and the early evidence, while not conclusive, is intriguing. The theory rests on the peculiar nature of fictional experience. To read a novel is to inhabit, for a time, the consciousness of another person — to feel, from the inside, what it is to be someone quite unlike ourselves, shaped by different circumstances and pursuing different ends. This, the argument runs, is precisely the mental act that empathy requires: the imaginative projection into another's point of view. Several studies lend the idea support. Readers of literary fiction, some research has found, score higher on tests measuring the ability to infer others' emotions and mental states. One interpretation is that fiction provides a kind of practice ground, where, through repeated immersion in other minds, we hone the social skills that real relationships demand. Caution is warranted, however. The studies show correlation, not proof of cause; it may be that naturally empathetic people are simply more drawn to fiction in the first place, rather than fiction making them so. Disentangling the two is notoriously difficult. Yet even if the effect proves modest, the underlying idea retains its force. In an age of division, when we increasingly struggle to understand those unlike ourselves, anything that strengthens our capacity to imagine other lives is worth taking seriously. A story, on this view, is not merely an escape from the world, but a quiet rehearsal for living more humanely within it. The novel, on this account, is not a luxury or a mere amusement, but one of the most powerful instruments human beings have ever devised for the slow and difficult work of genuinely understanding one another.

questions.prompt: What does the theory linking fiction and empathy rest on?
options: The fact that reading is relaxing.
That reading lets us inhabit another's consciousness, the very act empathy requires.
The idea that fiction is literally true.

questions.prompt: What did some studies find?
options: That readers of literary fiction score higher on tests of inferring others' emotions.
That fiction has no effect at all.

questions.prompt: Why is caution warranted?
options: Because the studies show correlation, not proof of cause.
Because no one reads any more.

questions.prompt: What is a story, on this view?
options: Merely an escape from the world.
A quiet rehearsal for living more humanely within the world.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 阅读使我们进入他人意识、恰是共情所需)、细节(B 文学小说读者推断他人情绪得分更高)、细节(B 仅为相关、非因果证明)、主旨(B 故事是更人道地生活的预演)。

sets.passage: For most of the twentieth century, urban planners designed cities around the automobile. Roads were widened, distances stretched, and the simple act of walking became, in many places, inconvenient or even dangerous. Only recently have planners begun to recognise the costs of this approach, and to rediscover the value of the 'walkable' city. The benefits of walkability are surprisingly far-reaching. The most obvious is health: residents of walkable neighbourhoods get more physical activity, almost effortlessly, simply because the shops, schools and services they need lie within easy reach. But the advantages extend further. Walkable areas tend to support local businesses, as pedestrians, unlike drivers speeding past, pass shop windows and stop on impulse. They are better for the environment, replacing car journeys with emission-free walking. And, more subtly, they foster a sense of community: people who walk encounter their neighbours, and streets full of pedestrians feel safer and more alive than those given over to traffic. Designing for walking, however, requires more than pavements. It demands a fine grain of interconnected streets, a mixing of homes, shops and workplaces so that destinations are close together, and an attention to the human scale — shade, seating, and a sense of safety — that invites people to linger rather than hurry through. The car, of course, will not disappear, nor should it. But the rediscovery of walkability reflects a deeper shift in how we think about cities: away from the idea of a city as a machine for moving vehicles efficiently, and towards the older, wiser idea of a city as a place for people. A walkable city, in the end, is simply a city that has remembered who it is for. To design for the pedestrian, then, is to design for the human being rather than the machine, and to insist that a city be measured above all by the quality of the lives that are actually lived within its streets.

questions.prompt: How were cities designed for most of the twentieth century?
What benefits does walkability bring?
options: Health, support for local business, and environmental and community benefits.

questions.prompt: What does designing for walking require?
options: Interconnected streets, mixed uses, and attention to the human scale.

questions.prompt: What deeper shift does walkability reflect?
options: Seeing a city as a machine for cars.
A shift towards seeing a city as a place for people.
The idea that cities are unimportant.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 围绕汽车设计)、细节(B 健康/本地商业/环境/社区多重益处)、细节(B 需互联街道、混合功能、人性尺度)、主旨(B 从'为车'转向'为人'的城市观)。

sets.passage: There is a peculiar paradox at the heart of innovation: the organisations that most prize success often produce the least of it. The reason lies in their relationship with failure. Genuine innovation, by its nature, requires experiment, and experiment, by its nature, mostly fails. An organisation that cannot tolerate failure cannot, therefore, innovate. This truth runs counter to deep human instincts. Failure is painful and embarrassing; our natural response is to avoid it, to punish it, and to hide it when it occurs. In many companies, the employee whose bold project fails is quietly sidelined, while the one who plays it safe and achieves modest, predictable results is rewarded. The rational response of everyone watching is to stop taking risks — and with them, to stop innovating. The most innovative organisations, by contrast, have learned to treat failure differently: not as something to be punished, but as information to be valued, an inevitable and necessary by-product of trying new things. They distinguish carefully between the failure of a careless mistake and the 'intelligent failure' of a well-designed experiment that simply did not work — and they celebrate, or at least forgive, the latter. This does not mean indulging incompetence or pursuing failure for its own sake. The aim is to fail well: to experiment cheaply, to fail fast and learn quickly, and to extract from each failure the knowledge that brings eventual success closer. The deepest lesson is counter-intuitive but profound: that to increase the rate of success, an organisation must, paradoxically, increase its tolerance for failure — for the two, far from being opposites, are inseparable partners. The organisations that punish every failure, however well-intentioned they may be, end by punishing the very experimentation on which all genuine innovation silently depends, and so quietly guarantee their own slow stagnation.

questions.prompt: What paradox lies at the heart of innovation?
options: That organisations which most prize success often produce the least of it.

questions.prompt: Why does experiment mostly fail?
options: Because experiment, by its very nature, mostly fails.

questions.prompt: How do the most innovative organisations treat failure?
options: As information to be valued, an inevitable by-product of trying new things.

questions.prompt: What is the deepest lesson of the passage?
options: That to increase success, an organisation must paradoxically increase its tolerance for failure.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 最重成功的组织反而产出最少创新)、细节(B 实验本性多以失败告终)、细节(B 视失败为有价值的信息、试新之必然副产品)、主旨(B 欲增成功须提高对失败的容忍)。

sets.passage: We rarely think of trust as an economic resource, yet it may be among the most important. Every transaction, from buying a coffee to signing a contract, rests on a foundation of trust — the expectation that others will keep their word, that money will be honoured, that we will not be cheated. Where this trust is strong, economies flourish; where it is weak, they struggle. The reason is that trust dramatically reduces what economists call 'transaction costs' — the time, effort and money spent on guarding against being cheated. In a high-trust society, a handshake can seal a deal; people cooperate readily with strangers, confident that agreements will be honoured. In a low-trust one, every transaction must be hedged with contracts, guarantees and inspections, all of which are expensive and slow. Distrust, in effect, is a tax on everything. Trust also enables the complex cooperation on which modern prosperity depends. A sophisticated economy involves vast chains of people who will never meet, each relying on the others to play their part. Such cooperation is possible only where a general expectation of trustworthiness prevails. This helps explain a striking and well-documented pattern: that societies with higher levels of social trust tend to be richer, and to have better-functioning institutions, than those with lower levels. Trust, it seems, is a kind of invisible infrastructure, as vital to an economy as its roads and ports, yet far harder to build and easier to destroy. Once squandered — through corruption, dishonesty, or broken promises — it is slow and difficult to rebuild, which is why the most prosperous societies guard it so carefully. A society rich in trust can dispense with much of the costly machinery of contracts, locks and inspectors that suspicion everywhere demands, and it is this hidden saving, more than any natural resource, that often quietly separates the prosperous nations from the poor.

questions.prompt: Why is trust described as an economic resource?
options: Because it dramatically reduces transaction costs, the cost of guarding against being cheated.

questions.prompt: What is distrust, in effect?
What well-documented pattern is mentioned?
options: That societies with higher social trust tend to be richer, with better institutions.
That trust is irrelevant to wealth.

questions.prompt: What is trust compared to?
options: An invisible infrastructure, as vital as roads and ports.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 大幅降低交易成本)、细节(B 不信任是对一切的'税')、细节(B 高社会信任社会更富、制度更健全)、词义/主旨(B 信任是如道路港口般重要的隐形基础设施)。

sets.passage: Meditation, long associated with religious and spiritual traditions, has in recent decades entered the mainstream, and with it has come scientific scrutiny. Stripped of its religious associations, meditation is, at its core, a form of mental training — the deliberate practice of directing and sustaining attention — and a growing body of research suggests that, like physical training, it can produce measurable effects. Studies indicate that regular meditation can reduce stress and anxiety, improve focus, and enhance emotional regulation. More striking still, brain-imaging research suggests that sustained practice may actually alter the physical structure of the brain, strengthening regions associated with attention and emotional control — a concrete illustration of the brain's plasticity. The mechanism is, in a sense, simple. Much of our distress arises not from events themselves but from our reactions to them — the anxious thoughts, the spiralling worries, the rumination over past and future. Meditation trains us to observe these mental events with a certain distance, recognising a thought as merely a thought, rather than being swept away by it. With practice, this creates a small but valuable space between stimulus and response, a moment in which we can choose how to react rather than reacting automatically. None of this makes meditation a cure-all, as some of its more enthusiastic promoters imply. Its benefits are real but modest, and it demands sustained effort to achieve them. Yet in a culture of constant distraction and stimulation, the capacity it cultivates — to be present, to observe one's own mind, to respond rather than merely react — may be unusually valuable, a quiet counterweight to the restless tempo of modern life. In an age that prizes constant stimulation and ceaseless activity, the simple discipline of sitting still and attending to the present moment may turn out to be not a retreat from life at all, but a way of meeting it more fully and more calmly.

questions.prompt: What is meditation at its core?
options: A form of mental training that directs and sustains attention.

questions.prompt: What does brain-imaging research suggest?
options: That sustained practice may actually alter the physical structure of the brain.
That the brain is permanently fixed.

questions.prompt: What does meditation create between stimulus and response?
options: A small space in which we can choose how to react rather than reacting automatically.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's view of meditation's benefits?
options: That it is a miraculous cure-all.
That they are real but modest, demanding sustained effort.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 本质是引导并维持注意力的心智训练)、细节(B 持续练习或改变大脑结构)、细节(B 在刺激与反应间创造可选择的空间)、主旨/态度(B 益处真实但有限、需持续努力)。

sets.passage: Why do experts so often make poor teachers, and why is it so hard to explain something we know well to someone who does not? A large part of the answer lies in a subtle cognitive bias known as the 'curse of knowledge': once we know something, we find it extraordinarily difficult to imagine not knowing it. The phenomenon is easily demonstrated. Ask someone to tap out the rhythm of a famous song while another person tries to guess it. The tapper, hearing the full tune in their head, is astonished when the listener, hearing only a series of disconnected taps, fails to identify it. The knowledge in the tapper's mind makes it almost impossible for them to appreciate how little information the listener actually has. This bias has wide-ranging consequences. The expert, fluent in the concepts and vocabulary of their field, genuinely forgets what it was like not to understand them, and so explains in terms a beginner cannot follow, skipping the very steps the novice most needs. Writers baffle readers, specialists confuse the public, and the knowledgeable, with the best of intentions, talk past those they wish to help. Overcoming the curse of knowledge is difficult precisely because it operates below awareness; we cannot simply will ourselves to un-know what we know. The remedy lies in deliberate effort: in seeking feedback from those who do not yet understand, in remembering our own past confusion, and in the humble recognition that what is obvious to us may be a mystery to others. Good communication, on this view, is less a matter of knowing one's subject than of imagining one's audience. The true teacher, then, must perform a difficult act of imagination, climbing back down to the place where the learner actually stands and recalling, against the grain of long mastery, what it once felt like not to know.

questions.prompt: What is the 'curse of knowledge'?
options: The difficulty, once we know something, of imagining not knowing it.

questions.prompt: What does the tapping demonstration show?
options: That the tapper's knowledge makes it hard to appreciate how little the listener actually hears.
That music is easy to identify.

questions.prompt: What consequence does the bias have?
options: Experts forget what not understanding is like and explain over beginners' heads.

questions.prompt: What is good communication, on this view?
options: Less a matter of knowing one's subject than of imagining one's audience.
Using as much jargon as possible.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 知道后便难以想象不知道的状态)、细节(B 敲击者难体会听者获得的信息有多少)、细节(B 专家忘了不懂的感受、讲解超出初学者)、主旨(B 良好沟通在于想象听众而非只懂主题)。

sets.passage: In a busy, demanding world, the ability to do several things at once — to 'multitask' — is often prized as a valuable skill. We answer emails during meetings, glance at our phones while talking to friends, and switch rapidly between tasks, proud of our productivity. Yet a substantial body of research suggests that multitasking, as we imagine it, is largely a myth, and an expensive one. The fundamental problem is that the human brain cannot, in fact, attend to two demanding tasks simultaneously. What we call multitasking is really rapid switching, the mind darting back and forth between tasks. And this switching is far from free. Each time we shift our attention, there is a cognitive cost — a small delay as the brain reorients itself — and these costs accumulate. Studies consistently find that people performing several tasks at once do each of them more slowly, and more poorly, than they would have done one at a time. Worse, the costs are partly hidden. Multitaskers typically feel that they are being highly productive, even as they are in fact being less so; the sense of busyness is mistaken for genuine accomplishment. Habitual multitasking may even, some research suggests, gradually erode our capacity for the sustained concentration that demanding work requires. The implication runs against the grain of modern working life, with its constant interruptions and its glorification of busyness. It suggests that the path to doing more, and doing it well, lies not in juggling everything at once, but in the unfashionable discipline of doing one thing at a time — of giving a single task our full, undivided attention, and finishing it before turning to the next. What we proudly call multitasking, the research finally suggests, is in truth merely rapid switching, and it leaves us doing many things distractedly and badly rather than any single thing genuinely well.

questions.prompt: What is multitasking really, according to the research?
options: Genuinely doing two things at once.
Rapid switching between tasks, not true simultaneity.

questions.prompt: What is the cost of switching attention?
options: A cognitive cost — a delay as the brain reorients — that accumulates.
There is no cost at all.

questions.prompt: Why are the costs partly hidden?
options: Because multitaskers feel productive, mistaking busyness for genuine accomplishment.
Because there are no real costs.

questions.prompt: What does the research imply about doing more?
options: That we should multitask even more.
That the path to doing more well lies in doing one thing at a time with full attention.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 实为任务间的快速切换)、细节(B 每次切换有累积的认知成本)、细节(B 把忙碌错当成就、成本被掩盖)、主旨(B 做得多且好在于一次专注一件事)。

sets.passage: Walk through any market and you will find the past for sale: vinyl records, film cameras, mechanical watches, and reissued products of decades gone by. In an age of relentless technological progress, the popularity of these older, often less convenient things presents a puzzle. Why, when superior modern alternatives exist, do so many people seek out the technologies of the past? Part of the appeal is sensory and tangible. A vinyl record must be handled, its sleeve admired, its side turned; a mechanical watch can be wound and felt ticking on the wrist. Such objects engage us physically in a way that the frictionless, immaterial experiences of the digital age do not. In a world where music and images have become weightless streams of data, there is a satisfaction in owning something solid. But the deeper appeal, many suggest, is emotional. These objects carry associations — with our own youth, or with an idealised past we never knew — and to use them is to feel connected to that past. In a present that often feels uncertain and overwhelming, the past offers a comforting solidity, and consuming its artefacts is a way of touching it. There is, perhaps, an irony here. The very technologies that have made our lives so convenient have also, for some, made them feel strangely thin and rushed, and the appetite for the analogue past is in part a reaction against this. The popularity of old things may thus tell us less about the things themselves than about what their owners feel is missing from the present — a longing not really for the past, but for the qualities of solidity, slowness and meaning that it has come to represent.

questions.prompt: What puzzle does the passage address?
options: Why people seek out older technologies despite superior modern alternatives.

questions.prompt: What is part of the sensory appeal of old objects?
options: That tangible objects engage us physically, unlike immaterial digital experiences.

questions.prompt: What is the deeper appeal of these objects?
options: An emotional connection to the past and its associations.

questions.prompt: What does the popularity of old things really tell us?
options: That old things are simply better.
What their owners feel is missing from the present — solidity, slowness and meaning.
That modern technology has failed completely.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 有更好现代替代品却仍追旧技术之谜)、细节(B 实体之物带来数字体验所无的身体参与)、细节(B 更深的吸引是与过去的情感连接)、主旨(B 反映人们觉得当下缺失的实在感/慢/意义)。
